content.assessment: Admissions-process reform without structural change leaves the underlying geographic distribution of 6-/8-year tracks untouched, meaning the network-level concentration of elite provision in Budapest and rural centers persists. This lens has limited traction on the proposal's core mechanism (exam/preparation-access reform), since it operates independent of school siting, transport, or capacity; however, network evidence indicates the tracks' rarity and concentration mean admissions-process changes cannot expand geographic access even if they alter who is selected within existing sites.
opportunities: Could be paired with network-level data on rural teacher shortages to target preparation-access interventions where capacity is weakest
strengths: Does not require new school construction, transport routes, or capacity expansion since existing sites are retained
threats: Church and private maintainers operating outside central authority may be geographically concentrated in ways that further limit the reach of any admissions redesign
weaknesses: Does not address the geographic concentration of 6-/8-year tracks in Budapest and a few regional centers, so admissions reform cannot improve rural access to these track types

Network rarity of 6-/8-year provision limits how much admissions-process reform alone can equalize opportunity, since students outside catchment areas cannot access reformed admissions regardless of process design
